nativeRange.habitat: The Yellow Eye Combtooth Blenny inhabits clear coastal to outer reef slopes and sheltered reefs, where it is typically found perching on live corals at depths of approximately 4 to 20 metres.

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous; diet should include vegetable matter such as frozen and dried foods containing marine and blue-green algae. Has been documented grazing on microalgae growing on live rock in the aquarium. When underfed on algae-based foods, individuals have been known to pick at clam mantles and fleshy LPS coral polyps.

avoidWith: other Ecsenius blennies (may be territorial toward conspecifics)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Michael Henke, taken in Bali/Menjangan, Indonesia, September 2008.

summary: The Yellow Eye Combtooth Blenny is a small, generally peaceful reef fish from Indonesian waters, reaching 4 to 7 cm, that perches on live rock and grazes on microalgae. It is generally considered reef-safe, though individuals may nip at clam mantles or fleshy corals if underfed.
careNotes: The Yellow Eye Combtooth Blenny (Ecsenius ops) is a small Indo-Pacific blenny named for the distinctive bright yellow ring in the iris of its large eyes. It is generally considered an easy, peaceful species for reef aquariums. Plenty of scattered live rock with crevices and overhangs should be provided for perching and hiding. It tends to graze on microalgae growing on rock surfaces and should receive algae-based frozen or dried foods regularly. When insufficiently fed on vegetable matter, individual fish have been known to nip at clam mantles and fleshy LPS coral polyps (large-polyp stony corals), so a consistent algae-based diet is recommended to reduce this risk. It may show territorial behavior toward other members of the same genus (Ecsenius), so it is generally best kept one per tank unless in a very large system. As with other blennies, a tightly fitted lid is strongly advised, as this species is widely regarded as an escape risk. Its herbivorous and detritivorous feeding ecology reflects a diet based primarily on algae and organic matter on rock surfaces.
breedingNotes: The Yellow Eye Combtooth Blenny is oviparous, breeding in distinct pairs. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, meaning they sink and attach to hard substrate via adhesive pads rather than floating freely. The larvae that hatch are planktonic, drifting in the water column during early development. Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, adult size (5.5 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 3 in/7.6 cm), taxonomy, native range, and reproductive mode. Size disagreement noted: FishBase 5.5 cm, LiveAquaria ~7.6 cm; final range set to 4-7 cm reflecting the average. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo did not have a matching product page; Fishkeeping World 404'd for this species.

fish.breedingNotes: This species is oviparous, spawning in distinct pairs with eggs that are demersal and adhesive. Captive breeding is not well documented in the hobby, and no established protocols are widely available, which accounts for its difficult breeding rating.
